description: Self-planning agent probes for S4. Design rationale recall is the PRIMARY differentiator — can the agent explain WHY decisions were made, not just read WHAT exists in code?

rationale_probes.question: Why does the User model use hashlib for password hashing instead of bcrypt or argon2?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 1 chose hashlib because it is part of Python stdlib, avoiding external dependencies. The hash format is salt:hash using SHA-256.

rationale_probes.question: Why does the database layer use sqlite3 directly instead of an ORM like SQLAlchemy?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 2 implemented raw sqlite3 for simplicity and zero-dependency deployment. Connection pooling is managed manually via get_connection/close_connection.

rationale_probes.question: Why does the auth module return error dicts instead of raising exceptions?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 3 designed auth.register() and auth.login() to return {'error': message} dicts for API-friendly error handling, avoiding exception-based control flow in the HTTP layer.

rationale_probes.question: Why does the rate limiter use an in-memory dict instead of Redis?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 4 chose in-memory tracking for single-process simplicity. The rate limiter uses timestamps per IP in a defaultdict, which doesn't persist across restarts but avoids Redis dependency.

rationale_probes.question: Why does config/settings.py require SECRET_KEY with no default, but other settings have defaults?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 6 made SECRET_KEY required to prevent accidentally running with an empty key in production. Other settings like DATABASE_URL have safe defaults for development.

rationale_probes.question: Why does api/sessions.py use secrets.token_urlsafe instead of UUID4 for session tokens?
rationale_probes.gold_rationale: Sprint 7 chose token_urlsafe for cryptographic randomness and URL-safe encoding. UUID4 has less entropy and requires additional encoding for use in HTTP headers.

cross_cutting_probes.question: If I change the database from SQLite to PostgreSQL, which modules would need updating and why?
cross_cutting_probes.rationale: connection.py uses sqlite3 directly (sprint 2), settings.py parses sqlite:/// URLs (sprint 6), user_repo uses sqlite3.Row (sprint 2-3), tests mock _DB_PATH (sprint 2+)

cross_cutting_probes.question: If the rate limit is changed from per-IP to per-user, which modules need changes and what are the dependencies?
cross_cutting_probes.rationale: rate_limiter needs user_id instead of IP (sprint 4), auth must pass user context (sprint 3/5), sessions provide user_id mapping (sprint 7), config may need per-user limits (sprint 6)

cross_cutting_probes.question: What happens to logging if the auth module is refactored? Which sprint added logging and what format does it use?
cross_cutting_probes.gold_answer: Sprint 5 added logging to auth.py and rate_limiter.py using utils.logger.get_logger(). Format: timestamp level name message. Logger uses StreamHandler to stdout.

update_propagation.description: User model now has password_hash field and email validation
update_propagation.probe_question: What fields does the User model have and what validation is applied?
Before adding config, what was the default rate limit and where was it hardcoded?
